← the Atlas

kolu-tui — a real TUI version of kolu, remote-first

Features·seedling·proposed·JUN 30, 2026

Not another raw single-daemon CLI — kolu-tui is kolu itself in the terminal, in kolu's own two-pane shape: the DOCK rail on the left (the selected host's terminals, which you switch between), the live terminal you type into as the main panel, and a HOST SELECTOR at the top of the rail to switch which host you're on (one host at a time, kinda like tmux sessions). It EMBEDS @kolu/sutra (own process, own state — Option B, not a thin renderer of a running kolu-server) connected to one host, and renders that layout with OpenTUI (the SolidJS-reconciler terminal framework, the lib that powers opencode; runs under Bun). The rail reuses the shared agentProjection folds (agentBucket / agentUrgency / agentPaintClass), so kolu-tui is a new renderer of the Dock-fleet-mirror contract, not a re-derivation. Built remote-FIRST as the de-risk milestone — connect to a real ssh host, PTY, reconnect, boot re-dial-re-adopt, adoption, host-switching, basic awareness — and it MUST exercise the pulam dial (awareness + fs/git ride one getHostSession(binary pulam) surface, so proving awareness proves fs/git's transport for free). No Code tab, byte preview, paste/upload or transcript. Phases K1-K3.

Contents

  • User-facing — kolu’s Dock, in the terminal
  • Architecture — embed the substrate · render with OpenTUI · reuse the Dock’s folds
  • What it proves vs skips
  • Implementation & phases — OpenTUI/Bun, the agentProjection reuse, K1–K3
  • See also

kolu itself, in the terminal. kolu already has two raw single-daemon CLIs — kaval-tui reads PTY bytes, pulam-tui reads awareness — and each is a scriptable face of one daemon. kolu-tui is a different animal: it embeds the sutra substrate (own process, own state — like kaval-tui is its own process, not a thin client of a running kolu-server) connected to one host, and lays out kolu’s own two-pane shape — the Dock rail on the left, the live terminal you type into as the main panel, and a host selector to switch which host you’re on. A tmux-for-agents that is the kolu UI, minus the Code browser. It’s the second consumer that proves sutra graduated, and it’s built remote-first as the de-risk milestone for the finale.

User-facing — kolu’s Dock, in the terminal

The layout is kolu’s own, in the terminal: the Dock rail on the left — the selected host’s terminals, each with its live agent badge — and the main panel is the live terminal you type into. The same two-pane shape as kolu-web (Dock + canvas), rendered by OpenTUI. At the top of the rail sits a host selector: you’re on one host at a time (kinda like tmux sessions), and switching hosts (h) swaps the whole rail to that host’s terminals.

kolu-tuiOpenTUI · embeds @kolu/sutra
◧ ssh nix@prod ▾
●kolu · claude
◜api · codex
○infra · vim
✓web · claude
nix@prod · ~/kolu · feat/dial-ssh · claude ● awaiting you
⏺ Bash(cargo test)
⎿ test result: ok. 128 passed; 0 failed
I've got the ssh dial passing. Want me to wire the
reconnect backoff next, or open the PR first?
> open the PR 
↑↓ switch term · ⇥ focus · h switch host · c create · type → main pane · ● kolu awaiting you

Two kinds of switching, cleanly separated: ↑/↓ (or j/k) moves the terminal selection within the current host and the main pane follows; the host selector (h) changes which host you’re on. ⇥ toggles focus between navigating the rail and typing into the terminal, so keystrokes go where you mean. Because a surface is scoped to one host, a terminal’s id is just its bare ptyId — no composite key, no host threaded through every action.

The badges are not a re-implementation: they are the shared agentProjection folds the web Dock and pulam-web already use — agentBucket (working · awaiting · waiting), with the order≠colour split intact (a just-finished waiting agent ranks idle but paints awaiting). kolu-tui is a new renderer of the Dock ⇄ fleet-mirror contract, importing the folds, never re-deriving a switch over agent states.

One host at a time — the cross-host glance is pulam-web's

kolu-tui shows one host’s terminals, switched by the selector — it does not mix local and remote terminals in one rail. That’s deliberate, and it’s what lets the id stay a bare ptyId (no composite key). The cross-host “which agent needs me across ALL my hosts, at once” view already exists in the right place: pulam-web, the browser fleet board that spans every host, sorted needs-you-first. kolu-tui is the workbench for the selected host (tmux-style); the fleet glance is pulam-web’s.

Create on the current host — c; it’s sutra’s create(opts) on the connected host, one keystroke, and the new terminal lands in the rail focused in the main pane:

kolu-tui · c → create (on nix@prod)
cmd? claude
# spawning on ssh nix@prod (the connected host)…
✓ added to the Dock · ~/kolu · a3f9…c21 (survives the link)

What it deliberately is NOT — the scope line that keeps it honest

kolu-tui is the Dock rail + the terminal pane + basic awareness, one host at a time. It has no Code-tab fs/git browser, no binary/image preview, no paste/upload, no transcript export, no recency clock of its own (recency is kolu’s remembered fact, absent from the memoryless snapshot a dial serves). Those are full-client surface work that lands in F-REMOTE, not here. The smaller surface is the point: prove the hard transport/lifecycle volatility without the web rendering riding along.

Architecture — embed the substrate · render with OpenTUI · reuse the Dock’s folds

kolu-tui is its own process, composed of three parts that map cleanly onto kolu’s existing boundaries:

  1. The embedded sutra substrate, connectSutra(host), owns the lifecycle for the selected host — create(opts), attach(id), dial + reconnect + boot re-adopt — exactly as kolu-server uses it. This is Option B and it is load-bearing: if kolu-tui were a thin renderer of a running kolu-server, nothing would graduate and it would be a second UI, not a second consumer. Because it embeds and dials the host itself, it is the proof.
  2. OpenTUI renders the two-pane layout — specifically its SolidJS reconciler @opentui/solid (compile-time JSX → renderables, no virtual DOM, the same Solid model kolu’s web client already uses), so the Dock rail and the terminal pane can be expressed in the framework kolu is built in. OpenTUI is the right reuse: it’s the SolidJS-native TUI framework (the lib that powers opencode), and pulam-tui already road-tested it in-repo.
  3. The shared agentProjection folds give the rail its meaning — agentBucket / agentUrgency / agentPaintClass from @kolu/terminal-workspace, imported (never re-derived), making kolu-tui the next renderer of the Dock ⇄ fleet-mirror contract.

The pulam-dial requirement — the proof condition, not an afterthought

kolu-tui must dial the connected host’s pulam for the rail’s awareness badges, and that is deliberate. Awareness and fs/git are procedures on the same terminalWorkspaceSurface, served by the same pulam binary, reached through the same getHostSession({ host, binary: "pulam" }) pool key (packages/surface-nix-host/src/hostSession.ts:911-929; pulam-tui dials it the same way, packages/pulam-tui/src/hostConnect.ts:56-64). So a kolu-tui that exercises the pulam dial for the Dock has by construction exercised the exact transport fs/git rides — same ssh provision, same pulam --stdio seam, same typed link. Even with no Code tab, kolu-tui proves the fs/git transport; what F-REMOTE adds is the rendering. Skip the pulam dial (e.g. fold only the in-process local producer) and the TUI stops being a de-risk — it becomes a second UI. This condition is what makes the milestone worth more than a product.

The three terminal-side clients, kept distinct:

kaval-tui pulam-tui kolu-tui
Dials kaval, binary: kaval — ptyHostSurface pulam, binary: pulam — terminalWorkspaceSurface embeds sutra, which dials both for the connected host
Sees raw PTY bytes derived meaning (agent/git/PR), no bytes a live terminal pane and the Dock rail
Renderer raw passthrough (Node) scriptable text/NDJSON (Node, no OpenTUI) full-screen OpenTUI two-pane (@opentui/solid, Bun)
Proves PTY transport over ssh awareness producer over ssh the sutra orchestration + both dials, end-to-end

pulam-tui shed OpenTUI; kolu-tui is where it earns its place

pulam-tui was a full OpenTUI dashboard, then reverted to a thin Node CLI (#1582) once pulam-web became the browser fleet board — a raw status/watch snapshot has no place for a full-screen renderer. kolu-tui is the opposite case: a two-pane Dock+terminal you switch and type in genuinely needs a layout/widget/reactivity engine, and hand-rolling one would violate the repo’s “prefer external libraries” rule. So OpenTUI comes back here, where it’s load-bearing rather than redundant.

What it proves vs skips

The milestone is honest only if the path it exists to prove is the one a done-criterion can only meet by hitting it — so name it exactly.

Proves (transfers verbatim to the full client):

  • The sealed sutra substrate end-to-end — every op routed by bare ptyId through the connected host’s endpoint, no host-seam bypass possible.
  • Connecting to a real ssh host and driving its remote PTY into the terminal pane over the kaval dial; reconnect / blip survival (HostSession backoff + the durable kaval --stdio daemon outliving the link).
  • Boot re-dial-re-adopt and adoption — reconnecting to a host and re-adopting its surviving PTYs, exercised on a real remote host.
  • Host-switching — the selector tears down one connectSutra and opens another (local ↔ remote), the connection-context model the whole plan rests on.
  • The rail fed over the pulam dial — which, per the condition above, proves the awareness and fs/git transport the full client inherits, and exercises the shared agentProjection as a TUI renderer.

Skips (deliberately — full-client surface, lands in F-REMOTE): the fs/git Code tab UI, the binary-preview byte route, paste/upload, transcript export, rich awareness rendering + restore UX. kaval-tui already proves raw PTY; kolu-tui’s added value is the kolu orchestration, the two-pane Dock+terminal, and the dial coverage — never the rendering F-REMOTE owns.

The de-risk, in one line

kolu-tui lights the hardest, least-reversible third of remote terminals — dial, transport, reconnect, host-lifecycle, adoption, host-switching — behind kolu’s real Dock, with no web rendering, and ships as a usable product while doing it. The full client then inherits a proven substrate and only has to add its own pixels.

Implementation & phases — OpenTUI/Bun, the agentProjection reuse, K1–K3

The library decisions are made (so an implementer doesn’t have to guess):

  • Renderer: OpenTUI via @opentui/solid. SolidJS reconciler, so the Dock rail and terminal pane are authored in the same framework as the web client; reuse the presentation-neutral agentProjection folds outright, and reuse the web Dock’s component logic where it’s portable (the structure + the folds port; DOM/CSS specifics do not — confirm the component-reuse depth at K1).
  • Runtime: Bun — OpenTUI’s native Zig core needs it (the same reason pulam-tui ran under Bun before reverting). sutra dials kaval/pulam daemons over sockets/ssh rather than spawning native PTYs in-process, so the Bun process carries no native PTY dependency; the kaval/pulam daemons stay as they are.
  • Honest caveat: OpenTUI is pre-1.0 (“not ready for production” upstream). It’s the right SolidJS-native bet and has in-repo precedent, but pin a version and treat a renderer-API break as a real K1 risk — the sutra/awareness work behind the Dock is unaffected either way.

Three increments, each shippable, each remote-from-the-start. They come after the sutra extraction.

kolu-tui — remote-first, on the embedded sutra substrate✓ shipped · ▶ do next · ◐ build-clean · ○ todo · → drill in
○K1 · embed + the Dock — link @kolu/sutra; render the two-pane Dock rail + terminal + host selector on OpenTUI (@opentui/solid); switch/type on a connected host (local AND a remote)needs SUBSTRATE
○K2 · survive the link — reconnect + blip survival · boot re-dial-re-adopt · adoption for a real remote host · host-switching via the selectorneeds K1
○K3 · live rail badges over the pulam dial — agentProjection (working/awaiting/waiting) per row, proving the awareness + fs/git transportneeds K2 · the proof condition
○→ F-REMOTE · the full web tile inherits the proven substrate + the host-selector dropdown→ finale
  • K1 — embed + the Dock. The skeleton that proves Option B: kolu-tui embeds sutra (own process, under Bun) and renders the two-pane Dock rail + terminal + host selector on OpenTUI; create/kill, switch-between-terminals (rail selection swaps the main pane), and raw typing into the focused pane work on a connected host — local and a remote you can select. Done: a remote terminal appears in the rail, is selected/typed-into/killed through sutra; the PTY survives a detach.
  • K2 — survive the link. Reconnect, boot re-dial-re-adopt, adoption, host-switching — sutra’s hard volatility under real ssh conditions. Done: kill the link mid-session → the pane reconnects and the remote PTY is intact; restart kolu-tui → it re-dials the host and re-adopts surviving PTYs; the host selector switches local ↔ remote and each shows its own terminals.
  • K3 — live rail badges over the pulam dial. Dial the connected host’s pulam, fold the snapshot stream through the shared agentProjection into each rail row’s bucketed badge + live ●. Done: a remote agent’s working → awaiting shows in the rail while you work in another pane — proving the pulam transport (and thus fs/git’s) that F-REMOTE’s Code tab will ride.
